postgres.title: PostgreSQL Architecture
postgres.description: Understanding PostgreSQL's process and memory architecture

postmaster.title: Postmaster - The Main Supervisor
postmaster.description: The postmaster is PostgreSQL's parent process that controls all database operations. It listens for client connections on port 5432 (default) and manages all child processes.
postmaster.keyPointText: If the postmaster crashes, the entire PostgreSQL instance stops. It's the single point of control for the database cluster.

backend.title: Backend Processes
backend.description: Each client connection gets its own dedicated backend process. This provides process isolation - if one backend crashes due to a bad query, other connections remain unaffected.
items: Memory: Each backend has its own memory space
Limitation: High memory usage with many connections

backgroundWriter.title: Background Writer
backgroundWriter.description: Writes dirty buffers from shared memory to disk periodically

checkpointer.title: Checkpointer
checkpointer.description: Creates recovery checkpoints to minimize recovery time

walWriter.title: WAL Writer
walWriter.description: Flushes Write-Ahead Log buffers to disk for durability

autovacuum.title: Autovacuum
autovacuum.description: Removes old row versions and prevents table bloat

sharedBuffers.title: Shared Buffers - The Data Cache
sharedBuffers.description: Shared buffers are PostgreSQL's main data cache, storing frequently accessed table and index pages in memory to avoid expensive disk I/O.

workMem.title: work_mem
workMem.description: Memory allocated for query operations like sorting and hash joins. Each operation can use up to this amount.
workMem.importantText: Complex queries may use work_mem multiple times (once per sort/hash operation). If exceeded, data spills to disk, causing major slowdowns.

maintenanceWorkMem.title: maintenance_work_mem
maintenanceWorkMem.description: Used by VACUUM, CREATE INDEX, and other maintenance operations. Larger values speed up maintenance tasks.

wal.title: Write-Ahead Log (WAL)
wal.description: WAL is a sequential log of all database changes. It's the foundation of PostgreSQL's durability and replication.
howItWorksItems: Change is written to WAL buffer
On COMMIT, WAL flushed to disk
Later, data pages updated on disk
If crash occurs, replay WAL to recover

dataFiles.title: Data Files
dataFiles.description: PostgreSQL stores table data in heap files, organized into 8KB pages. Each table and index is stored in separate files, split into 1GB segments.

mvcc.title: MVCC - Multi-Version Concurrency Control
mvcc.keyConcept: Key Concept: Multiple row versions enable high concurrency
mvcc.keyConceptDescription: Instead of locking rows, PostgreSQL keeps multiple versions. Readers see their transaction's snapshot, writers create new versions.
howItWorksItems: Each row has xmin (created) and xmax (deleted) transaction IDs

items.title: Process per connection
items.description: provides isolation but requires connection pooling at scale

items.title: Shared buffers
items.description: cache hot data in memory to minimize disk I/O

items.title: WAL ensures durability
items.description: and enables crash recovery and replication
